chunks.text: Stereo. H C J D A 38 JUDGMENT SHEET IN THE LAHORE HIGH COURT, LAHORE. (JUDICIAL DEPARTMENT) Criminal Appeal No.6763-J/2025. Ayaz Adil. Vs. The State, etc. JUDGMENT DATE OF HEARING: 31.10.2025. APPELLANT BY: M/S Faseeh Ullah Khan, Abdul Hayee & Khadim Hussain Tahir Hashmi, Advocates. STATE/COMPLAINANT BY: Mr. Waqas Anwar, Ms. Noshe Malik & Dr. Muhammad Usman, Deputy Prosecutors General: Ms. Asmat Parveen, Deputy District Public Prosecutor. AMICI CURIAE: Mr. Sohail Shafiq Chaudhry & Rai Usman Bashir, Advocates: Ms. Nuzhat Bashir, Deputy Prosecutor General and Mr. Kamran Adil, Deputy Inspector General of Police. MUHAMMAD AMJAD RAFIQ, J:- Trial of Ayaz Adil along with his father Sami Ullah, sister Maria Sami and mother Bushra Bibi by the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Faisalabad in case FIR No.34 dated 12.01.2024 under sections 302, 311, 201, 34 PPC Police Station Sahianwala District Faisalabad on the charge of qatl-i-amd of his sister Amna Sami was concluded through judgment dated 18.12.2024, whereby while acquitting the co-accused, he was convicted under section 302 (b) Pakistan Penal Code 1860 (“the PPC”) and sentenced to imprisonment for life along with payment of Rs.10,00,000/- as compensation under section 544A the Code of Criminal Procedure 1898 (“the Code”) to the legal heirs of Amna Sami, the deceased; in default to further undergo six months’ simple imprisonment. Benefit of section 382B of the Code was extended. Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 2 Being aggrieved by his above conviction and sentence, the appellant has filed instant criminal appeal. 02. Upon receiving information about the alleged intentional murder (qatl-i-amd) of Amna Sami by her brother, Ayaz Adil, with the involvement of other family members, the complainant, SI Muhammad Maqsood Shahid, along with other police officials, reached the scene on 12.01.2024 at 12:30 p.m.

Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 2 Being aggrieved by his above conviction and sentence, the appellant has filed instant criminal appeal. 02. Upon receiving information about the alleged intentional murder (qatl-i-amd) of Amna Sami by her brother, Ayaz Adil, with the involvement of other family members, the complainant, SI Muhammad Maqsood Shahid, along with other police officials, reached the scene on 12.01.2024 at 12:30 p.m. They observed that the crime scene had been freshly washed, and the body of Mst. Amna Sami was lying on a cot. Local residents were openly stating that Ayaz Adil had killed his sister on the pretext of honour, while the father was lamenting that his daughter had committed suicide. It was also reported that the mother and sister had attempted to destroy evidence by cleaning the place of occurrence. 03. On completion of all legal formalities, report under section 173 of the Code was sent to the Court, accused were charge sheeted, to which they pleaded not guilty and claimed to be tried. 04. Prosecution examined complainant, Muhammad Maqsood Shahid SI/I.O. (PW.3), Khadim Hussain 524/C (PW.4) for res gestae evidence, whereas, Dr. Salma Mumtaz (PW.5) conducted postmortem examination of dead body of the deceased, and rest of the witnesses were formal in nature. Prosecution has given up Ahsan Javed 4542/C, Bilal Hassan, Naveed Babar 4039/C and Zain computer operator being unnecessary and after tendering report of PFSA (Exh.PN) closed its evidence. Statements of accused persons under Section 342 of the Code were recorded wherein they denied the prosecution case, neither opted to record their statement under Section 340 of the Code nor to produce defence evidence. Ultimately, three accused were acquitted and accused/appellant was convicted and sentenced as forecited. 05. Learned counsel (s) for the appellant claimed that there is not a single evidence available on record against the accused/appellant that could justify his conviction but learned trial court swayed in emotion passed the judgment on surmises and conjectures; while learned

Ultimately, three accused were acquitted and accused/appellant was convicted and sentenced as forecited. 05. Learned counsel (s) for the appellant claimed that there is not a single evidence available on record against the accused/appellant that could justify his conviction but learned trial court swayed in emotion passed the judgment on surmises and conjectures; while learned Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 3 Prosecutors supported the judgment being perfect in the circumstance to curb the menace of honour killing. The heated stances adopted by the parties, both to counter the menace of honour killings and to address concerns of false implication in such cases, prompted the Court to frame the following question through its order dated 16.10.2025: “While hearing of this appeal, a question arises that though burden of proof remains on the prosecution in every situation yet for cases involving ‘qatl on karokari’ or ‘honour killing’, wherein ocular account is usually absent if near relations or inmates of the house do not come forward, does Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, which contains Islamic Provisions, create any room for shifting of such burden on the accused for his appearance as a witness under section 340 of Cr.PC in disproof of charges or allegations, or clarification of circumstances about the death of the deceased, if his presence is established at the place of occurrence; and if he refuses to answer or concedes such facts in his statement under section 342 Cr.PC, would it also be considered, a circumstance or evidence in the light of Article 17 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order 1984, and is sufficient to record conviction against him while relying on ‘probability formulae’ introduced through Article 2 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order 1984. 2. Further in such cases, what sort of relevant evidence could be collected by the police and be transformed into admissible format by the prosecution. Learned counsel (s) for the appellant and learned Deputy Prosecutor General are directed to come prepared on these points. 3.

Further in such cases, what sort of relevant evidence could be collected by the police and be transformed into admissible format by the prosecution. Learned counsel (s) for the appellant and learned Deputy Prosecutor General are directed to come prepared on these points. 3. The above questions involve explanation of different provisions of law and a deep study for a way forward, therefore, Mr. Sohail Shafiq Chaudhry & Rai Usman Bashir, Advocates, Ms. Nuzhat Bashir, DPG and Mr. Kamran Adil, DIG, are appointed as amici curiae to assist the Court on above legal queries. Any other person who would like to assist the Court is also welcomed. 4. Adjourned to 24.10.2025.” On 24 October 2025, the amici curiae — Ms. Nuzhat Bashir (Deputy Prosecutor General), Mr. Sohail Shafiq Chaudhry and Rai Usman Bashir (Advocates), and Mr. Kamran Adil (Deputy Inspector General of Police) — along with Ms. Noshe Malik (Deputy Prosecutor General) and Ms. Asmat Parveen (Deputy District Public Prosecutor), presented their submissions in response to the assistance sought through the Court’s order dated 16 October 2025. To address the queries raised by the Court, particularly those mentioned in paragraph Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 4 2 of the order dated 16 October 2025, a reference was also sent to the Prosecutor General Punjab. His response has since been received. 06. The central issue before the Court was whether any established rule of evidence exists for dealing with cases of honour killing committed secretly within the confines of a home, and what form of proof should be required in such circumstances. The Court also considered whether ancillary principles—such as the burden of proof and the standard of proof—should be re-examined or redefined in order to improve the likelihood of securing convictions in such cases. 07. Ms. Nuzhat Bashir (DPG) addresses that there is a recurring tension in honour-killing cases: families often stay silent, evidence is thin, and courts wonder whether an accused person’s silence or weak explanation can help fill the gaps and whether such silence or admission could constitute evidence or circumstance under Article 17 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order 1984 (“the QSO 1984”).

Ms. Nuzhat Bashir (DPG) addresses that there is a recurring tension in honour-killing cases: families often stay silent, evidence is thin, and courts wonder whether an accused person’s silence or weak explanation can help fill the gaps and whether such silence or admission could constitute evidence or circumstance under Article 17 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order 1984 (“the QSO 1984”). She articulated that as per Article 117 of the QSO 1984., the burden of proof lies on the person who asserts a fact and in criminal cases, it is the prosecution; whereas as per sections 342 & 340 of the Code, the accused has the right, not the obligation, to testify. His failure to testify cannot by itself create guilt but can support an inference when other circumstances exist. While reading some excerpts from paragraphs No. 22 to 37 of a case reported as “Afzaal Hussain Shah Vs the State” (1991 P Cr. L J 113 (DB)), she pointed out that the non-availability of direct evidence does not absolve the Court of its onerous obligation of determining the fact as to whether the death of the deceased was the result of a felonious act and of its further duty to fix the guilt of the person responsible for the same. The law has always considered the circumstantial evidence as a lawful guide in the administration of criminal justice, and circumstances established beyond reasonable doubt could furnish a basis for decision, better than any other kind of evidence. She stated that the bare denial of the petitioner about knowledge of occurrence and not offering any Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 5 explanation would be a strong corroborative circumstance if the ocular account is available, but denial alone cannot be a form of proof. In this respect, she has placed reliance on a case reported as “Muhmmad Akram Vs The State” (2003 SCMR 855). While referring case reported as “Saeed Ahmad Vs. the State” (2015 SCMR 710), she emphasized on the concept of alternate theory and stated that in above judgment Supreme Court of Pakistan pursuant to Article 122 of the QSO 1984 held that special knowledge of circumstances by the accused requires him to at least give explanation as to why he has not informed the police about his wife's murder, not taking her to the hospital, not participating in her last rites and disappearing for a long period of two months.

In this respect, she has placed reliance on a case reported as “Muhmmad Akram Vs The State” (2003 SCMR 855). While referring case reported as “Saeed Ahmad Vs. the State” (2015 SCMR 710), she emphasized on the concept of alternate theory and stated that in above judgment Supreme Court of Pakistan pursuant to Article 122 of the QSO 1984 held that special knowledge of circumstances by the accused requires him to at least give explanation as to why he has not informed the police about his wife's murder, not taking her to the hospital, not participating in her last rites and disappearing for a long period of two months. She had also touched the Indian jurisdiction and referred that in the case of “State of Andhra Pradesh v. Gangula Satya Murthy” (AIR 1997 SC 1588) the Indian Supreme Court had deemed a failure to offer an explanation as adverse to the accused. She said that similar principles more or less were also discussed in following judgments from Indian and Pakistani jurisdictions; “State of Karnataka v. Khaja Hussain” (1982 SCC 456); “Ram Gulam Chaudhary v. State of Bihar” (2001 SCC 311); “State of Rajasthan v. Kashi Ram” (AIR 2007 SC 144); “State of Rajasthan v. Jaggu Ram” (AIR 2008 SC 982); “Arshad Mehmood v. State” (2005 SCMR 1524); “Roheeda v. Khan Bahadur” (1992 SCMR 1036); “Muhammad Ijaz alias Jajj v. The State” (2025 SCMR 1591); “Nasrullah v. State” (2017 SCMR 724); “Asad Khan v. State” (PLD 2017 Supreme Court 681). Finally, she suggested that evidence in the form of proof under Article 17 of QSO 1984 when juxtaposed with adverse inference, it becomes imperative for the accused to appear as a witness under section 340 of the Code to give explanation in order to avert such adverse inferences. 8. The learned amicus curiae, Mr. Kamran Adil Deputy Inspector General of Police commended the government’s efforts for introducing the amendments in year 2004 and 2016 to the PPC aimed at curbing the menace of honour killings. He noted, however, that Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 6 despite these legislative reforms, communities remained hesitant to report such offences to the police. Consequently, the Police Practical Work (PPW) initiative required the police to assume the role of complainant in honour-killing cases. Since the implementation of this measure, the incidence of such crimes has shown a marked decline, as reflected in the crime statistics of the province.

Consequently, the Police Practical Work (PPW) initiative required the police to assume the role of complainant in honour-killing cases. Since the implementation of this measure, the incidence of such crimes has shown a marked decline, as reflected in the crime statistics of the province. Cases of honour killings in Punjab, year 2020 to 2025 Year 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 No. of cases 203 168 172 122 162 96 He while supporting the views of Ms. Nuzhat Bashir DPG stated that a clear legislation is required in such cases for an evidentiary requirement, which the India has taken care of in new legislation titled “the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023” through section 117 (Presumption as to abetment of suicide by a married woman) and section 118 (Presumption as to dowry death). He while referring cases reported as “Saeed Ahmad vs. the State” 2015 SCMR 710 “Raja Khurram Ali Khan vs. Tayyaba Bibi” (PLD 2020 Supreme Court 146); “Muhammad Abass vs. The State” (PLD 2020 Supreme Court 620) submitted that court must adhere to concept of evidential and legal burden on the accused in such cases. However, where prosecution case is wholly unreliable, there appears no question of shifting the evidential burden on the accused, because inferences can only strengthen already proved case but cannot substitute proof. The Supreme Court of Pakistan has held in a case reported as “Wajhat v. Gul Draz” (2019 SCMR 1451) that mere presence and silence of accused is not enough for conviction. According to his view the chapter relating to “General Exceptions” in the PPC cannot be made applicable on the legislation introduced through Qisas & Diyat Ordinance 1990 which is based on Islamic principles, and could only be synchronized if chapter XVI of the PPC is interpreted in the light of Islamic Injunctions as laid down in the Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 7 Holy Quran and Sunnah per mandate of section 338F of the PPC or through Presidential Order under Article 267 of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan 1973.

According to his view the chapter relating to “General Exceptions” in the PPC cannot be made applicable on the legislation introduced through Qisas & Diyat Ordinance 1990 which is based on Islamic principles, and could only be synchronized if chapter XVI of the PPC is interpreted in the light of Islamic Injunctions as laid down in the Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 7 Holy Quran and Sunnah per mandate of section 338F of the PPC or through Presidential Order under Article 267 of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan 1973. In cases of honor killing, usually there is no evidence at all, therefore, Islamic Injunctions on such situation are very clear and also were part of recommendations of Islamic Ideology Council at the time when Qisas & Diyat Ordinance was being legislated. He referred Report of the Standing Committee of the Religious Affairs on Draft Law (Enforcement of Qisas & Diyat) Ordinance 1981; according to which where offender committing qatl is unknown and wali of victim suspects involvement of any person, he is required to take oath (qasamat) before a court competent to try the offence or any judicial officer authorized by such court, and refusal to take oath procures detention of suspect until he makes confession or tell the name of real offender. In order to explain the types of evidence to be collected by the police during investigation in honour killing cases, he suggested (i) medical evidence (signs & nature of injury) (ii) Forensic and physical evidence (recovery of weapon, DNA profiling, mobile phone data, CDR & location) (iii) circumstantial evidence (conduct of accused & last seen) (iv) Motive and social context evidence (family hostility & prior disputes) (v) witness testimonies and statement (statements of neighbours & friends) (vi) documentary or digital trail (electronic data like chats, call logs, text messages & CCTV footages). 9. Mr. Sohail Shafiq Chaudhary Advocate, the learned amicus curiae, has emphasized on the Article 122 read with Article 129 of the QSO 1984 for a shift of burden on inmates of the house to explain the circumstances culminated into death of victim, and on failure court may draw inference from the evidence produced by the prosecution in order to convict them on the basis of probability formulae introduced through Article 2 of the QSO 1984.

Mr. Sohail Shafiq Chaudhary Advocate, the learned amicus curiae, has emphasized on the Article 122 read with Article 129 of the QSO 1984 for a shift of burden on inmates of the house to explain the circumstances culminated into death of victim, and on failure court may draw inference from the evidence produced by the prosecution in order to convict them on the basis of probability formulae introduced through Article 2 of the QSO 1984. In this respect he also placed reliance on cases reported by Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 8 Pakistani Courts as “Muhmmad Akram Vs The State” (2003 SCMR 855); “Saeed Ahmad Vs. the State” (2015 SCMR 710): “Nasarullah Vs. The State” (2017 SCMR 724): “Asad khan Vs. The State” (PLD 2017 Supreme Court 681): “Muhammad Ijaz alias Jujj vs. The State” (2025 SCMR 1591), and by the Indian Courts as “State of Karnataka vs. Khaja Hassan” [1982 SCC 456]: “Ram Ghulam Chaudhary vs. The State of Bihar” [2001 SCC 311]: “State of Rajasthan vs. Kashi Ram” (AIR 2007 SC 144): “State of Rajasthan vs. Jaggu Ram” [AIR 2008 SC 982]. While explaining the requirement of evidence which must be collected in honour killing cases, he emphasized on the spot recoveries including biological stains, digital, chemical, documentary and impression evidence (finger prints, footwear, tyre impression, bite marks etc.) and while referring judgment of Supreme Court of Pakistan in Noor Muqadam Case, he appreciated the silent witness theory based on the evidences in the form of CCTV footages whose admissibility is regulated under Electronic Transaction Ordinance 2002 and Article 164 of the QSO 1984. 10. Rai Usman Bashir Advocate, the learned amicus curiae while dilating upon Article 117 to 120 claims that the initial legal burden is on prosecution to prove the murder against the accused, and it never shifts in any situation. He was of the view that provisions of the QSO 1984 relating to relevancy of facts are good enough to prove the case against the accused even on the basis of circumstantial evidence which is hall mark of Article 17 of the QSO 1984, and Article 2 of the QSO 1984 merely aids the court to evaluate the circumstantial evidence and it does not lower the standard of proof from “beyond reasonable doubt”.

Rai Usman Bashir Advocate, the learned amicus curiae while dilating upon Article 117 to 120 claims that the initial legal burden is on prosecution to prove the murder against the accused, and it never shifts in any situation. He was of the view that provisions of the QSO 1984 relating to relevancy of facts are good enough to prove the case against the accused even on the basis of circumstantial evidence which is hall mark of Article 17 of the QSO 1984, and Article 2 of the QSO 1984 merely aids the court to evaluate the circumstantial evidence and it does not lower the standard of proof from “beyond reasonable doubt”. According to him, probability may supplement the evidence but can never substitute it. Section 342 & 340 of the Code do not compel the accused to testify or explain the circumstances against him and his silence may at the best be treated as a relevant circumstance but it cannot be deemed as a confession or independent proof of guilt. The principle “nemo tenetur se ipsum accusare” (no Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 9 one is bound to incriminate himself) protects the accused’s right to silence. In this respect he placed his reliance on cases reported as “Niaz vs. The State” (PLD 1960 Supreme Court 387): “Rehmat alias Rhaman alias Waryam alias Badshah vs. The State” (PLD 1977 Supreme Court 515); “Wajahat vs. Gul Daraz and another” (2019 SCMR 1451); “Shukat Hussain vs. The State” (2022 SCMR 1358); “Woolmington v. DPP” (1935 AC 462). While referring Article 17 of the QSO for guidance from the Islamic injunctions with respect to number and quality of evidence, he stated that the Holy Quran declares; “O you who believe! Stand out firmly for justice as witness to Allah, even though it be against yourself or your kindred” (Surah An-Nisa 4:135). The Prophet (SAW) stated; “al-bayyinah ‘ala man iddaa wal- yamin ‘ala man Ankara” (the burden of proof is upon claimant, and the oath is upon denier), and another Hadith commands “Idra’ūl- Ḥudūd bi-l-Shubuhāt” (avoid the punishments for hudood crimes in cases of doubt). He submitted that these sources affirm that in Islam, no punishment may be inflicted on mere suspicion (Zann) or probability. Certainty (yaqeen) and clear proof (bayyinah Saheehah) are indispensable to uphold justice.

He submitted that these sources affirm that in Islam, no punishment may be inflicted on mere suspicion (Zann) or probability. Certainty (yaqeen) and clear proof (bayyinah Saheehah) are indispensable to uphold justice. Dr. Tanzeel-ur-Rehman in Principles of Islamic Jurisprudence explains that in Islamic criminal law, yakin (certainty) cannot be displaced by shakk (doubt) — hence the maxim “al-yaqīn la yazūlu bi’al-shakk” is in place. Similarly, A. K. Brohi in Fundamentals of Islamic Jurisprudence states that presumptions in Sharia are secondary evidentiary tools and never autonomous grounds for conviction. Further, Justice (R) Dr. Fida Hussain’s Commentary on the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order underscores that Article 122 dealing with facts especially within knowledge must be applied narrowly to avoid undermining the constitutional protection of presumption of innocence. The commentary refers to “Muhammad Nawaz v. State” (PLD 1982 Supreme Court 139) and “Abdul Majeed v. State” (PLD 1991 Supreme Court 949) as key precedents maintaining that the evidential shift arises only after the prosecution’s proof of essential facts. While showing comparative Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 10 analysis, learned amicus curiae said that the UK case of “Woolmington v. DPP” AC 462 remains the cornerstone of common law, reaffirmed in “R v. Lambert” 3 WLR 206, that the burden to prove guilt is immutable. In Canada, “R. v. Lifchus” 3 SCR 320 clarified the reasonable doubt standard; and in Australia, “R v. Hillier” HCA 13 confirmed that circumstantial proof must exclude all rational hypotheses consistent with innocence. The Iranian Penal Code (Book 5, Article 160) and Qarār-e-Dalilī principles similarly emphasize that conviction in hudood and Qisas offences must rest on yakin (certainty), not ghalabat al-zann (preponderance of probability). Jurists like Imam al-Ghazali (Al- Mustasfa fi Usul al-Fiqh) and Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya (I’lam al- Muwaqqi’in) wrote extensively that suspicion (zann) is inadmissible as a substitute for proof (yakin), and conviction based on incomplete evidence is a violation of divine justice.

The Iranian Penal Code (Book 5, Article 160) and Qarār-e-Dalilī principles similarly emphasize that conviction in hudood and Qisas offences must rest on yakin (certainty), not ghalabat al-zann (preponderance of probability). Jurists like Imam al-Ghazali (Al- Mustasfa fi Usul al-Fiqh) and Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya (I’lam al- Muwaqqi’in) wrote extensively that suspicion (zann) is inadmissible as a substitute for proof (yakin), and conviction based on incomplete evidence is a violation of divine justice. The learned amicus curiae, however conceded that if prosecution establishes a reasonable presumption of guilt by credible corroborative evidence, particularly in honour killing or qatl on karo-kari cases where ocular account is rare, the accused is expected to discharge a limited evidential burden by offering a plausible explanation. However, this does not shift the legal burden, nor does it dilute the constitutional and Islamic requirement that guilt must be proved beyond reasonable doubt (al- yaqīn la yazūlu bi’al-shakk — certainty is not overruled by doubt). This harmonized approach, supported by statutory law, case law, and Islamic scholarship, ensures that justice is dispensed in accordance with both the Constitution and Sharia, balancing the rights of the accused with the demands of truth and public order. His recommendations were as follows; 1. Statutory Codification: Parliament may consider introducing a specific provision in the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 or Criminal Procedure Code, 1898, defining a “presumption of knowledge and explanatory burden” applicable in domestic or honour-related deaths. 2. Judicial Test (Proposed Three-Step Formula): Step 1: Prosecution to establish foundational facts — unnatural death, presence or control of accused, motive, and corroborative evidence. Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 11 Step 2: Evidential burden shifts to the accused under Article 122 QSO to explain facts peculiarly within his knowledge. Step 3: If no explanation is given, the court may apply the probability formula (Article 2 QSO) to evaluate the silence as a corroborative factor, not as substantive proof, ensuring the “beyond reasonable doubt” threshold is preserved. 3.

Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 11 Step 2: Evidential burden shifts to the accused under Article 122 QSO to explain facts peculiarly within his knowledge. Step 3: If no explanation is given, the court may apply the probability formula (Article 2 QSO) to evaluate the silence as a corroborative factor, not as substantive proof, ensuring the “beyond reasonable doubt” threshold is preserved. 3. Judicial Safeguards: Courts must ensure that any inference from silence is applied cautiously, only after prosecution establishes guilt presumptively. This balance preserves due process, ensures Islamic standards of proof, and promotes accountability without violating the accused’s fundamental rights. 11. Ms. Asmat Parveen, the learned prosecutor while focusing on the concept of circumstantial evidence derived from the phrase “such other evidence as the circumstances of the case may warrant” mentioned in Article 17 of the QSO 1984 has referred an evidential test known as ‘Panchsheel test’, practiced in Indian jurisdiction, enunciated through case reported as “Sharad Birdhichand Sarda Vs. State of Maharashtra” (AIR 1984 SC 1622) to give a go to the circumstantial evidence to help record conviction against the accused. According to her, above phrase indicates that a specific action or ruling should be determined by the unique facts and conditions of that particular situation. It gives the court or other authority the flexibility to take steps that are appropriate or necessary for that specific case. It prevents standardized decisions in scenarios where facts might require a different response and it gives flexibility and discretion to the court. She explained the situations which cater to the requirement of ‘as the circumstance of the case may warrant’, that include (i) admissibility of secondary evidence (ii) relevancy of other irrelevant facts (iii) admissibility of confessional statement. While explaining Panchsheel test, she outlined the quality of circumstantial evidence as under; 1. Fully Established: The circumstances must be proven beyond doubt. 2.

While explaining Panchsheel test, she outlined the quality of circumstantial evidence as under; 1. Fully Established: The circumstances must be proven beyond doubt. 2. Consistent with Guilt: Facts must align only with the hypothesis of guilt. 3. Conclusive Nature: Circumstances must be decisive, not just suggestive. 4. Exclusion of Other Hypotheses: All innocent explanations must be eliminated. Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 12 5. Complete Chain: Evidence must form an unbroken chain, leaving no reasonable doubt of innocence. 12. Ms. Noshe Malik, learned DPG was of the view that while applying probability formulae under Article 2 of the QSO 1984, the ratio/score must be 1:0 and not less than that. Meaning thereby prosecution must have some case, may be one strong presumption on the basis of probabilities in its favour but then corresponding inference in favour of accused’s innocence must be zero. No standard less than that would procure conviction. She while referring judgment of this court titled “Fayyaz Ahmad vs. The State” (2025 LHC 5942), submits that once prosecution establishes a presumption of guilt against the accused beyond reasonable doubt, on the basis of his presence at crime scene, control of situation, motive, and chain of events, then accused’s silence or failure to offer a plausible explanation may be considered a corroborative circumstance, helpful in recording conviction against him. She has also relied upon a case reported as “Mazhar Ali vs. The State” (2005 SCMR 523). While relying on judgment reported as “State of Madhya Pradesh vs. Balveer Singh” (2025 SCC online SC 390) stated that Indian Supreme Court has held that if there is no direct evidence, resolve is in Article 106 of the Evidence Act (122 of the QSO 1984). She regarded the honour killing a calculated offence involving a complete conspiracy that is for sure leaves traces or clue at crime scene, and offender cannot escape if the investigator and forensic team observe the crime scene keenly by associating the people from surroundings.

Balveer Singh” (2025 SCC online SC 390) stated that Indian Supreme Court has held that if there is no direct evidence, resolve is in Article 106 of the Evidence Act (122 of the QSO 1984). She regarded the honour killing a calculated offence involving a complete conspiracy that is for sure leaves traces or clue at crime scene, and offender cannot escape if the investigator and forensic team observe the crime scene keenly by associating the people from surroundings. She claimed that rumours in like offences usually have wings to fly which could be a best catch for a line of inquiry in such cases. To achieve that end she suggested that statement under section 161 of the Code must be video recorded, or these statements may be given a legal cover so as to use it as legal evidence. She also suggested that there must be some legislation that if honour killing is not proved then in alternative, unnatural death in a house must be a distinct offence to catch the inmates of the house. Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 13 13. The learned Prosecutor General represented through Dr. Muhammad Usman, learned DPG responded to the query of the court while splitting the subject into ‘probability section’, ‘special knowledge’ and discharge of burden by accused through statement under section 340 of the Code. For probability formulae, he described three standards of proof; (i) preponderance of probability (in civil cases), (ii) proof beyond reasonable doubt (criminal cases) (iii) clear and convincing proof (in civil cases involving allegation of criminal nature). According to him such three standards are in fact three different degrees of probability which cannot be expressed in mathematical terms and are to be evaluated under the circumstances of the particular case as provided in clause of Article 2 of the QSO 1984. In this respect reliance was placed on cases reported as “Salamt Ali and others versus Muhammad Din and others” (PLD 2022 Supreme Court 353) & “Said Wali versus Yaqoob Khan and another” (PLD 1983 Supreme Court 440). For facts especially in the knowledge of accused or if the case of accused attracts general exceptions, the arguments were advanced touching the Articles 121, 122 & 129 of the QSO 1984 and reliance was placed on cases reported as “Raja Khurram Ali Khan vs.

For facts especially in the knowledge of accused or if the case of accused attracts general exceptions, the arguments were advanced touching the Articles 121, 122 & 129 of the QSO 1984 and reliance was placed on cases reported as “Raja Khurram Ali Khan vs. Tayyaba Bibi” (PLD 2020 Supreme Court 146); “Raja Muhammad Afzal Khan vs. Ch. Manzoor Elahi and 6 others” (PLD 1975 Lahore 1276); “Muhammad Ijaz aias Jajj v. ILLUSTRATIONS (a) A is found killed in a locality or a mahallah inhabitted by several persons. The oath (qasamat) shall be taken from residents of such locality or mahallah. (b) A is found killed at a distance from two different localities. If A’s voice could be heard in both the localities, the oath shall be taken by the residents of the locality which is nearer to the place of occurrence. (c) A is found killed in a house. The oath (qasamat) shall be taken by the occupants of such house. (d) A is found killed in a vehicle consisting of one compartment. The oath (qasamat) shall be taken by the driver, the conductor and the passengers of such vehicle. (e) A is found killed in a vehicle having more than one compartment. The oath (qasamat) shall be taken by the passengers of the compartment where the dead body is found. (f) A is found killed in a vehicle having no other passenger except the victim. The oath (qasamat) shall be taken by the residents of the locality where the vehicle and the victim are found. 105. Before that it is essential to see what are the presumptions and the related standard of proof. 18. There are certain rules of evidence known as presumptions, which have an effect on the normal incidence of the burden of proof. A presumption is a rule of law which provides that if a party proves a certain fact (known as the primary fact) then another fact (the presumed fact) will also be taken to be proved, unless evidence is adduced by opponent to ‘rebut’ the presumption or in other words contradict the presumed fact. If the statute does not lay legal burden on accused to rebut such presumed fact, then at least it creates an evidential burden on him; therefore, presumed fact will be taken as proved unless the accused adduces some evidence to rebut the presumption, though he needs not go so far as to disprove it; which means he must explain the circumstances how he sees the existence of primary fact, and if he does so, the presumption disappears and normal burden of proof applies as if the presumption had never exist.

In the Majalla-tul-Ahkaam Adlia (the Ottoman Civil Code), Section 1741 deals with the admissibility and weight of circumstantial evidence (Qarinah), a decisive presumption. The core concept is that while circumstantial evidence is recognized and admissible in many cases, it generally must lead to a conclusion of guilt that is so strong that no other reasonable hypothesis can be inferred. The Majalla, drawing on traditional Hanafi jurisprudence, allows for the use of circumstantial evidence, especially in areas where direct proof (such as explicit eyewitness testimony of a specific number of male witnesses) is unavailable. Circumstantial evidence, denoting presumption, conjunction, linkage, and indication, is indirect evidence of relevant facts from which the existence of a fact in issue might be logically deduced. Section 1741 of the Majalla, in essence, stipulates the following principles regarding circumstantial evidence: • Caution against mere suspicion: A judge should not pass judgment based merely on wahm (baseless suspicion) or tahaddi (ambiguity/mere presumption). • Need for certainty: For circumstantial evidence to be admissible and reliable, it must lead to a conclusion that is certain (yaqin) or near-certain, leaving no room for reasonable doubt or alternative explanations of innocence. • Supporting evidence: Circumstantial evidence can be used to corroborate or support direct evidence, but on its own, its probative value is generally limited if an alternative hypothesis exists.

Whereas the standard of proof on basis of probability is inherent in our judicial system introduced through Article 2 of the QSO 1984 which says that “a fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists”. In legal proceedings, no specific number of facts are required to introduce the concept of probability. Instead, probability inherently underpins the legal standards of proof itself, which is applied to all the admitted evidence at the end of a trial to determine a fact. The “go” to probability is when there are sufficient relevant and admissible facts for a judge to reasonably weigh the likelihood of a proposition being true. Article 2 of the QSO 1984 merely aids the court to evaluate the circumstantial evidence and it does not lower the standard of proof from “beyond reasonable doubt” because probability may supplement the evidence but can never substitute it. However, the ‘Panchsheel test’ gives the court or other authority the flexibility to take steps that are appropriate or necessary for that specific case. It prevents standardized decisions in scenarios where facts might require a different response and it gives flexibility and discretion to the court. If accused does not clarify the impending allegations 1 or concedes his liability in his statement under section 342 of the Code that he has committed the offence, 2 it would certainly be considered, a circumstantial evidence in the light of Article 17 of the QSO 1984, and would fortify the consequential presumed facts which shall supplement to facts already established by the prosecution, providing justification to record conviction against him while relying on ‘probability formulae’ introduced through Article 2 of the QSO 1984. 36. Coming to the case in hand, the complainant Muhammad Maqsood Shahid, SI (PW-3), upon receiving spy information, reached the place of occurrence at about 12:30 p.m. and observed the dead body lying on a cot placed in a freshly washed courtyard. However, during spot recoveries, neither was the said cot taken into possession nor was any blood secured from any part of the house. Although a PFSA team was summoned to detect blood traces through scientific means, they collected only a few samples from the body of the deceased and did not recover any blood from the surroundings. The weapon of offence was also not found at the spot. Surprisingly, despite the courtyard having been washed, a spent shell was allegedly recovered from near the dead body. While the dead body was indeed found at the site, the prosecution failed to establish that the murder actually took place at that location. This constitutes first major dent in the prosecution’s case. 37. There was delay of around 5 hours in lodging the crime report; police arrived at 12.30.p.m. on 12.01.2024 but FIR was registered at 5.25 p.m. without any plausible justification, which raises suspicion about legal activity of police at the site.

This constitutes first major dent in the prosecution’s case. 37. There was delay of around 5 hours in lodging the crime report; police arrived at 12.30.p.m. on 12.01.2024 but FIR was registered at 5.25 p.m. without any plausible justification, which raises suspicion about legal activity of police at the site. Police have also not associated the persons from locality nor recorded their statements, who though were vocal enough to openly suspect the murder by the _________________________________________________________________ 1 (See Article 129 (h) of the QSO 1984: “Fayyaz Ahmad Vs The State” (2025 LHC 5942) 2 “ALI AHMAD Versus The STATE” (PLD 2020 Supreme Court 201) Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 39 present appellant, information whereof was picked up by a stool pigeon for transmission to the police. Complainant was also the investigating officer of the case, therefore was steering the facts partially and conceded during cross examination as under; “I did not join any private person. During the course of investigation, five persons of the locality namely Tayab Maqsood, Nawazish Ali, Bilal Hassan, Ghazanfar Ali and Fraz Ahmad joined the investigation proceedings. I did not record statements u/s 161 Cr.P.C. of the above said people of locality”. The above facts show inefficiency of police which has created another missing link in the case. 38. It was alleged that the deceased’s body was found inside the house, and rumours circulated that although the appellant had committed an honour killing, his father was attempting to portray the death as a suicide. The body was discovered lying on a cot in a freshly washed courtyard, indicating that someone from the family must have handled the body after death in order to place it there. In such a situation, the family members would naturally have touched or adjusted the body. However, the inquest report records that the deceased’s eyes and mouth were still open—an indication that no one from the household had attended to the body or been present at the time of death.

In such a situation, the family members would naturally have touched or adjusted the body. However, the inquest report records that the deceased’s eyes and mouth were still open—an indication that no one from the household had attended to the body or been present at the time of death. This condition of the body directly undermines the prosecution’s claim that the appellant committed qatl-i-amd. 39. Despite the PFSA team handed over certain samples to the complainant (PW-3), who acknowledged its receiving and deposit in PFSA on 16.01.2024 but the prosecution failed to produce any corresponding forensic report. Furthermore, Zeshan, the Junior Forensic Scientist who examined the crime scene and the dead body, was not produced as a witness to explain the nature of the samples collected. The complainant also claimed to have obtained the CDR of the accused, yet that too was never brought on record. He did not see the accused/appellant at the crime scene when he visited there, nor did Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 40 the prosecution establish that the accused resided at the relevant location with his parents. Consequently, the prosecution could not prove either the presence of the accused at the crime scene or his control over it. The alleged motive, an undesirable relationship of the deceased with another person, also remained unsubstantiated. Though complainant PW-3 claimed during cross examination as under; “CDR, regarding the contact of deceased with someone is also available on the record” But neither the call log of deceased was tendered into evidence nor police investigated about the person with whom deceased was in touch through cell phone, as conceded by the complainant PW-3 during his cross examination. 40. Moreover, at the site there were four accused persons who were carrying equal animus or grudge for committing honour killing yet prosecution has not produced evidence to single out the accused/appellant, and rest of the accused had already stood acquitted on the similar evidence.

Though complainant PW-3 claimed during cross examination as under; “CDR, regarding the contact of deceased with someone is also available on the record” But neither the call log of deceased was tendered into evidence nor police investigated about the person with whom deceased was in touch through cell phone, as conceded by the complainant PW-3 during his cross examination. 40. Moreover, at the site there were four accused persons who were carrying equal animus or grudge for committing honour killing yet prosecution has not produced evidence to single out the accused/appellant, and rest of the accused had already stood acquitted on the similar evidence. It was otherwise, requirement of Article 24 of the QSO 1984 which is as under; (Article 24 (b) of the QSO 1984) (b) The question is, whether A committed a crime. The circumstances are such that the crime must have been committed either by A, B, C, or D. Every fact which shows that the crime could have been committed by no one else and that it was not committed by either B, C or D, is relevant. 41. As noted above, although the courtyard had been freshly washed, the complainant (PW-3) nevertheless claimed to have recovered a spent shell from the exact spot where the dead body was lying. Furthermore, the alleged recovery of the pistol was shown after a delay of ten days, purportedly on the lead of the accused/appellant, from the haveli of Saif Ullah—adjacent to the place of occurrence. However, Saif Ullah himself was never produced by the prosecution to support or corroborate this recovery. In addition, PW-3 stated during his examination-in-chief that he arrested the accused/appellant Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 41 on 16.01.2024 and, on the very same day, deposited the spent shell at the PFSA office. This sequence of events creates a reasonable possibility that the recovery evidence could have been fabricated or manipulated. Consequently, the positive PFSA report loses its probative value and cannot safely be relied upon by the prosecution. 42. The complainant (PW-3) claimed during cross-examination that a confession by the accused and his mother existed on record for the purposes of Section 311 of the PPC. However, no such confession was ever produced in evidence. On the contrary, in his statement under Section 342 of the Code, the accused explicitly stated that Amna Sami had committed suicide but the prosecution did not produce any evidence to counter the accused’s claim of suicide, nor did they ask the medical officer any questions that could establish a contrary medical opinion.

However, no such confession was ever produced in evidence. On the contrary, in his statement under Section 342 of the Code, the accused explicitly stated that Amna Sami had committed suicide but the prosecution did not produce any evidence to counter the accused’s claim of suicide, nor did they ask the medical officer any questions that could establish a contrary medical opinion. Given this evidentiary gap, the prosecution failed to establish a case against the accused/appellant with a realistic prospect of conviction; therefore, he cannot be forced to discharge limited evidential burden. 43. For what has been discussed above, this Court has no doubt whatsoever to hold that prosecution has failed miserably to bring on record any iota of evidence which could in any manner connect the accused/appellant with the commission of offence, whereas, it was incumbent upon the prosecution to have proved its case against the accused beyond any reasonable doubt. Therefore, while relying on the cases reported as “QURBAN ALI versus The STATE” (2025 SCMR 1344); “MUHAMMAD IFTIKHAR Versus The STATE” (2022 SCMR 973); “WAJAHAT versus GUL DARAZ and another” (2019 SCMR 1451); “MUHAMMAD PERVAIZ versus THE STATE and other” (PLD 2019 Supreme Court 592); “SAJJAN SOLANGI Versus The STATE” (2019 SCMR 872);“STATE through Advocate-General, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Peshawar Versus HASSAN JALIL and others” (2019 SCMR 1154); “ASAD KHAN versus The STATE” (PLD 2017 Supreme Court 681) and “ABDUL MAJEED versus THE STATE” Crl. Appeal No.6763-J/2025. 42 (2011 SCMR 941), this appeal is allowed; conviction and sentence recorded by the learned trial Court against the appellant are set-aside. Appellant is acquitted of the charge. He be released forthwith from jail if not required to be detained in any other case. The case property, if any, be disposed of in accordance with law and the record of the learned trial Court be sent back immediately. 44.
